title: Variables & Types
description: Master JavaScript's variable declarations and type system — interviewers expect you to know let/const/var differences, type coercion traps, and how to check types reliably.

sections.title: let, const, and var
sections.explanation: In interviews, always default to `const` for values that don't change and `let` for loop counters or reassigned variables. Avoid `var` — it has function scope instead of block scope, which causes subtle bugs. Interviewers notice which declaration you reach for first.

exercise.prompt: Declare `maxSize` as a constant set to 100, and `count` with let set to 0. Increment count by 10 three times using a for loop with let. Print maxSize, then count.

validation.message: maxSize should be 100 and count should be 30 after three increments of 10
Use const for maxSize and let for count and the loop variable

exercise.hint: Replace the blanks: const for maxSize, let for count and i.

sections.title: Type coercion
sections.explanation: JavaScript coerces types implicitly with `==` and `+`. Interviews test whether you understand these traps. Always use `===` for strict equality. Know that `+` with a string concatenates, while `-`, `*`, `/` convert to numbers.

exercise.prompt: Print the results of: `5 + "3"`, `5 - "3"`, `1 === "1"`, `Number("99")`, and `String(256)` — each on its own line via console.log.

validation.message: Check each coercion result — 5+"3" is "53", 5-"3" is 2, 1==="1" is false
Use strict equality and explicit conversion functions

exercise.hint: Fill in: "3" for subtraction, "1" for strict comparison, "99" for Number, 256 for String.

sections.title: null and undefined
sections.explanation: JavaScript has two "empty" values. `undefined` means a variable was declared but never assigned; `null` is an intentional absence of value. In interviews, use `null` to represent "no result found" from a function and check with `=== null` or `=== undefined`.
sections.example:
let result;                // undefined
const empty = null;        // intentional empty

console.log(result === undefined); // true
console.log(empty === null);       // true

// == null catches both null and undefined
console.log(null == undefined);    // true
console.log(null === undefined);   // false

exercise.prompt: Declare `x` without assigning it. Declare `y` and assign null. Print whether x is undefined using ===, then print whether y is null using ===, then print whether null == undefined.

validation.message: x should be undefined, y should be null, and null == undefined is true
Use null for y and undefined for the comparison

exercise.hint: Assign null to y, and compare y === null.

sections.title: typeof operator
sections.explanation: The `typeof` operator returns a string identifying the type. Know the quirks: `typeof null` returns "object" (a historical bug), and `typeof` an array returns "object" — use `Array.isArray()` instead. Interviewers use this to test your JS depth.
sections.example:
console.log(typeof 42);          // "number"
console.log(typeof "hello");     // "string"
console.log(typeof true);        // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined);   // "undefined"
console.log(typeof null);        // "object" (JS quirk!)
console.log(typeof [1, 2]);      // "object"
console.log(Array.isArray([1])); // true

exercise.prompt: Print typeof for: 42, "hello", true, undefined, null. Then print Array.isArray([1, 2, 3]).

validation.expected:
number
string
boolean
undefined
object
true
validation.message: Remember typeof null is "object" — a famous JS quirk
Use typeof for primitives and Array.isArray for arrays

exercise.hint: Fill in: true, undefined, and [1, 2, 3] for the three blanks.
